description: Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.
For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.
No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.
The switch may only forward packets at a rate of 100 packets per second (pps) if they trigger an ICMP redirect.

Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

Ensure the cipher suite GCM-AES-128 is configured when AOS-CX is acting as a key server. By default, AOS-CX uses the more secure cipher suite GCM-AES-XPN-256 for establishing MACsec secure links. However, Comware/PVOS does not support the XPN cipher suite.
OSPFv2 and OSPFv3 do not support detailed LSA show commands.
Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.
REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.
DHCP Snooping (v4/v6) should not be enabled on the UBT client VLANs in either vlan extend and local vlan mode.

Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.
Only static VXLAN is supported.

Symptom: Switches observe a crash after applying specific configuration with the switchd_agent error. Scenario: In some scenarios after configuring Internet Protocol Flow Information Export (IPFIX) daemon switchd_agent crash may crash.
symptom: Switches observe a crash after applying specific configuration with the switchd_agent error.
scenario: s after configuring Internet Protocol Flow Information Export (IPFIX) daemon switchd_agent crash may crash.

description: Symptom: The webserver becomes unresponsive, but AFC is reachable. Restarting HTTPS and Nginx services does not recover connectivity. Netstat for ports 443 and 80 do not work as expected. Scenario: In some scenarios where there is an excessive memory usage and resource exhaustion, the nginx process is incorrectly terminated by the switch leading to webservice to become unresponsive.
symptom: The webserver becomes unresponsive, but AFC is reachable. Restarting HTTPS and Nginx services does not recover connectivity. Netstat for ports 443 and 80 do not work as expected.
scenario: s where there is an excessive memory usage and resource exhaustion, the nginx process is incorrectly terminated by the switch leading to webservice to become unresponsive.

description: Symptom: Unexpected multicast flooding on VLAN after upgrading from 10.10 to 10.13. Scenario: In some scenarios when upgrading from 10.10 to 10.13, IGMP flooding can be observed for VLANs that have IP IGMP snooping enabled. Workaround: Disable and re-enable IP IGMP snooping on affected VLAN.
symptom: Unexpected multicast flooding on VLAN after upgrading from 10.10 to 10.13.
scenario: s when upgrading from 10.10 to 10.13, IGMP flooding can be observed for VLANs that have IP IGMP snooping enabled.
workaround: Disable and re-enable IP IGMP snooping on affected VLAN.

description: Symptom: Some mac-auth clients show incorrect VLAN mapping after being authenticated. The show mac-address table detail command displays the default unauthenticated VLAN instead of the authorized VLAN. Scenario: In some cases during client authentication, client MAC is not aged out correctly after the client has been successfully authenticated and placed in authenticated VLAN.
symptom: Some mac-auth clients show incorrect VLAN mapping after being authenticated. The show mac-address table detail command displays the default unauthenticated VLAN instead of the authorized VLAN.
scenario: In some cases during client authentication, client MAC is not aged out correctly after the client has been successfully authenticated and placed in authenticated VLAN.

description: Symptom: sFlow generates an excessive number of logs. Scenario: When sFlow is enabled in the switch an excessive number of sFlow irrelevant logs are generated. these logs could be throttled.
symptom: sFlow generates an excessive number of logs.
scenario: When sFlow is enabled in the switch an excessive number of sFlow irrelevant logs are generated. these logs could be throttled.

description: Symptom: Intermittent MSTP/RPVST starvation messages observed coming from source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00. Scenario: When 3rd party legacy devices send packets with source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00 to CX devices intermittent BPDU starvation might be observed. Workaround: Configure ACL to drop invalid source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00 in all switches where end devices are connected.
symptom: Intermittent MSTP/RPVST starvation messages observed coming from source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00.
scenario: When 3rd party legacy devices send packets with source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00 to CX devices intermittent BPDU starvation might be observed.
workaround: Configure ACL to drop invalid source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00 in all switches where end devices are connected.

description: Symptom: The show aruba-central command displays the switch as connected but Central WebUI shows the connection failure error. Scenario: In some scenarios, the internal process in charge of initialize the connection to Central is not responding therefore the connection is refused in the Central WebUI.
symptom: The show aruba-central command displays the switch as connected but Central WebUI shows the connection failure error.
scenario: s, the internal process in charge of initialize the connection to Central is not responding therefore the connection is refused in the Central WebUI.

description: Symptom: The sticky-learn clients are not shown post reboot. Scenario: When port is down and switch is rebooted, entry is lost, and sticky-learn clients will not be shown.
symptom: The sticky-learn clients are not shown post reboot.
scenario: When port is down and switch is rebooted, entry is lost, and sticky-learn clients will not be shown.

description: Symptom: Cable diagnostics test does not return cable distance. Scenario: In some scenarios, cable diagnostic test feature is unable to return cable distance for pair of cables that are open.
symptom: Cable diagnostics test does not return cable distance.
scenario: s, cable diagnostic test feature is unable to return cable distance for pair of cables that are open.

description: Symptom: ERSPAN session not active and the show mirror <id> command display status field as external/driver_error. Scenario: When mirror destination endpoint is configured with sub-interface, the mirror session will not be active.
symptom: ERSPAN session not active and the show mirror <id> command display status field as external/driver_error.
scenario: When mirror destination endpoint is configured with sub-interface, the mirror session will not be active.

description: Symptom: Traffic loss observed for traffic matching with configured PBR policies in a VSX setup. Scenario: In some VSX configurations with PBR policies where the next hop is learned over MCLAGs, traffic loss can be observed after local links go down and new next hop is learned on different MCLAG. Workaround: Clear ARP.
symptom: Traffic loss observed for traffic matching with configured PBR policies in a VSX setup.
scenario: In some VSX configurations with PBR policies where the next hop is learned over MCLAGs, traffic loss can be observed after local links go down and new next hop is learned on different MCLAG.

description: Symptom: Proxy ARP and Local Proxy ARP functionality is not available.
symptom: Proxy ARP and Local Proxy ARP functionality is not available.

description: Symptom: Warning messages displayed for FAN tray status in the WebUI monitoring tool, despite having only one FAN tray installed. The switches were otherwise operating within normal parameters. Scenario: Single fan tray when monitoring via the WebUI. The issue was prominent in scenarios where only one FAN tray was connected.
symptom: Warning messages displayed for FAN tray status in the WebUI monitoring tool, despite having only one FAN tray installed. The switches were otherwise operating within normal parameters.
scenario: s where only one FAN tray was connected.

description: Symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a client configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to max MTU are incorrectly dropped and not mirrored. This applies to remote/tunnel mirrors as well. Workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.
symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror.
scenario: , the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.
workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled

description: Symptom: PTP may not work after reboot of a ptp-unaware neighbor device on setups with a large number of PTP clients. Scenario: This is issue is observed when the default PTP timers are modified and the device has a large number of PTP clients configured (over 20 per port). Workaround: For large scale PTP deployments, keep the PTP timers set to default.
symptom: PTP may not work after reboot of a ptp-unaware neighbor device on setups with a large number of PTP clients.
scenario: This is issue is observed when the default PTP timers are modified and the device has a large number of PTP clients configured (over 20 per port).
workaround: For large scale PTP deployments, keep the PTP timers set to default.

description: Symptom: Incorrect ACL seq# is applied in control plane ACL's. Scenario: When an ACLs with both comments and the log action are applied to the control plane, incorrect ACL seq# is configured for ACE. Workaround: Remove comments the ACL from the control plane.
symptom: Incorrect ACL seq# is applied in control plane ACL's.
scenario: When an ACLs with both comments and the log action are applied to the control plane, incorrect ACL seq# is configured for ACE.
workaround: Remove comments the ACL from the control plane.

description: Symptom: Traffic does not flow over MCLAGs, causing the Egress Block to remain unset. This results in traffic from the ISL being sent out over the MCLAG, potentially causing connected devices to receive duplicate packets. Scenario: Configuring an MCAG with AFC. Workaround: Create a new VLAN on the devices where the MCLAG was configured.
symptom: Traffic does not flow over MCLAGs, causing the Egress Block to remain unset. This results in traffic from the ISL being sent out over the MCLAG, potentially causing connected devices to receive duplicate packets.
scenario: Configuring an MCAG with AFC.
workaround: Create a new VLAN on the devices where the MCLAG was configured.

description: Symptom: The switch doesnt learn the MAC of a client that is denied on a secure port even when its moved to a trusted/non-secure port. Scenario: Configure port-access on a port (MAC_Auth/Dot1x/port-security). Setup the client MAC that is trying to onboard such that the MAC is denied. For example: Either by failing authentication or due to max client limit. Move such a client to a non-secure port. Observe the MAC table that the MAC is not moved to the non-secure port.
symptom: The switch doesnt learn the MAC of a client that is denied on a secure port even when its moved to a trusted/non-secure port.
scenario: Configure port-access on a port (MAC_Auth/Dot1x/port-security). Setup the client MAC that is trying to onboard such that the MAC is denied. For example: Either by failing authentication or due to max client limit. Move such a client to a non-secure port. Observe the MAC table that the MAC is not moved to the non-secure port.

description: Symptom: Traffic loss and ping failure due to ndmd crash. Scenario: Performing the clear arp command with a short interval.m
symptom: Traffic loss and ping failure due to ndmd crash.
scenario: Performing the clear arp command with a short interval.m

description: Symptom: Active IPv4 route is not displayed even though SVI is in up state. Scenario: When continuously removing and adding a VRF's for several iterations, the switch might fail to program new SVI's properly. Workaround: Avoid continuous adding or removing VRF and restart system daemon hpe-routing.
symptom: Active IPv4 route is not displayed even though SVI is in up state.
scenario: When continuously removing and adding a VRF's for several iterations, the switch might fail to program new SVI's properly.
workaround: Avoid continuous adding or removing VRF and restart system daemon hpe-routing.

description: Symptom: ops-switchd crash while collecting VXLAN diagdump or collecting support files all. Scenario: In some VXLAN scenarios with multiple LAGs and multiple overlay VLAN on those lags. After device reboot, the LAG interface could show incorrect VXLAN Programming. If the user attempts to remove VLANs, VNI-to-VXLAN configurations that belong to the affected LAGS, a ops-switchd crash might be observed. Workaround: Ensure LAG interfaces are up before configuring the VLANs for the lag.
symptom: ops-switchd crash while collecting VXLAN diagdump or collecting support files all.
scenario: s with multiple LAGs and multiple overlay VLAN on those lags. After device reboot, the LAG interface could show incorrect VXLAN Programming. If the user attempts to remove VLANs, VNI-to-VXLAN configurations that belong to the affected LAGS, a ops-switchd crash might be observed.
workaround: Ensure LAG interfaces are up before configuring the VLANs for the lag.

description: Symptom: Configuration push from Central fails due to a Dry Run error with the message systemNotReady. Scenario: In some scenarios, the internal Dry Run state may become invalid leading to failing when pushing the configuration.
symptom: Configuration push from Central fails due to a Dry Run error with the message systemNotReady.
scenario: s, the internal Dry Run state may become invalid leading to failing when pushing the configuration.

description: Symptom: VXLAN packets with UDP source port 69 are dropped at the wireless gateway. Scenario: Occurs when stub switch sends VXLAN packets with well-known ports instead of RFC7438 recommended range. Workaround: Move user traffic from vxlan vlan to a non-vxlan vlan.
symptom: VXLAN packets with UDP source port 69 are dropped at the wireless gateway.
scenario: Occurs when stub switch sends VXLAN packets with well-known ports instead of RFC7438 recommended range.
workaround: Move user traffic from vxlan vlan to a non-vxlan vlan.

description: Symptom: After switch reboot Management module 2 becomes the Active instead of Management Module 1. Scenario: During system boot, previous active management module might experience a delay in boot causing the previous standby management module to become new active management module.
symptom: After switch reboot Management module 2 becomes the Active instead of Management Module 1.
scenario: During system boot, previous active management module might experience a delay in boot causing the previous standby management module to become new active management module.

description: Symptom: Delay of ~30s observed when updating VLAN access config via snmpset and verifying via snmpget immediately after. Scenario: When snmpset changes one OID and snmpget reads another OID before the cache can result in not updated cache values for OID. Workaround: Use the CLI for VLAN access configuration instead of SNMP.
symptom: Delay of ~30s observed when updating VLAN access config via snmpset and verifying via snmpget immediately after.
scenario: When snmpset changes one OID and snmpget reads another OID before the cache can result in not updated cache values for OID.
workaround: Use the CLI for VLAN access configuration instead of SNMP.

description: Symptom: After boot system, interface link goes down after a 15 second delay, impacting convergence of other protocols for peer devices. Scenario: In a VSX system, the process of rebooting one of the VSX members this delay might be observed.
symptom: After boot system, interface link goes down after a 15 second delay, impacting convergence of other protocols for peer devices.
scenario: In a VSX system, the process of rebooting one of the VSX members this delay might be observed.

description: Symptom: High CPU observed with EVPN duplicate MAC dampening detected. Scenario: When EVPN duplicate MAC dampening is detected, the switch might generate additional unnecessary internal traps that can induce a high CPU.
symptom: High CPU observed with EVPN duplicate MAC dampening detected
scenario: When EVPN duplicate MAC dampening is detected, the switch might generate additional unnecessary internal traps that can induce a high CPU.

description: Symptom: NTP clients unable synchronize clock when attempting to authenticate from upstream switch. Scenario: Configure the switches as NTP conductor for multiple downstream switches with authentication enabled. Workaround: Remove and re-add the same trusted key once time is synced on the server.
symptom: NTP clients unable synchronize clock when attempting to authenticate from upstream switch
scenario: Configure the switches as NTP conductor for multiple downstream switches with authentication enabled.
workaround: Remove and re-add the same trusted key once time is synced on the server.

description: Symptom: Snmp-trap for link down of LAG member port is not received. Scenario: If the snmp-trap packet is only reachable through the LAG and a LAG member port is shutdown, the trap for the link down is not sent out properly. Workaround: Use inform option instead of trap.
symptom: Snmp-trap for link down of LAG member port is not received.
scenario: If the snmp-trap packet is only reachable through the LAG and a LAG member port is shutdown, the trap for the link down is not sent out properly.
workaround: Use inform option instead of trap.

description: Symptom: ACL fails to apply only the VSX secondary switch. ACL warning logs display the ! access-list ip <name> user configuration does not match active configuration. ! error. Scenario: In some scenarios when a config checkpoint restore or classifier list-update is performed in a VSX member, the ACL config will not be applied to the VSX secondary if the ACL config has vsx-sync is enabled. Workaround: Remove the ACL configuration and then re-apply the ACL.
symptom: ACL fails to apply only the VSX secondary switch. ACL warning logs display the ! access-list ip <name> user configuration does not match active configuration. ! error.
scenario: s when a config checkpoint restore or classifier list-update is performed in a VSX member, the ACL config will not be applied to the VSX secondary if the ACL config has vsx-sync is enabled.
workaround: Remove the ACL configuration and then re-apply the ACL.

content: Refer to the Approved Product Lists sites for the Common Criteria, FIPS 140-2 and DoDIN APL to obtain the product certification details. Products should be used as evaluated and defined in the respective configuration guides. Common Criteria: <https://www.niap-ccevs.org/Product/> FIPS 140-2: <https://csrc.nist.gov/Projects/Cryptographic-Module-Validation-Program/Validated-Modules/Search> DoDIN APL: <https://aplits.disa.mil/processAPList.action>

description: Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.
symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX.
scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features.
workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

description: Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected
symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable
scenario: 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected

section: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
task: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
procedure: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy).
Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config.
Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.
description: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

procedure: Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.

Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.

When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
description: Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
